verses.text: The men of Kirjath-jearim came, and fetched up the ark of the LORD, and brought it into the house of Abinadab in the hill, and sanctified Eleazar his son to keep the ark of the LORD. It came to pass, while the ark abode in Kirjath-jearim, that the time was long; for it was twenty years: and all the house of Israel lamented after the LORD. Guidance: A small hilltop town receives Israel's most sacred object. Abinadab — name unknown until now — opens his home; his son Eleazar (not the Levitical high priest of the same name, but consecrated for the role) keeps watch. The ark will rest here for twenty years until David retrieves it (2 Sam 6). The chapter's hinge: 'all the house of Israel lamented after the LORD.' The Hebrew verb is the sound of a baby's wail for absent mother — the whole nation finally misses God Himself, not just His ark. Two decades of quiet ache is what it took for Israel to want the LORD back. Sometimes the loudest worship begins with the longest silence.

The men of Kirjath-jearim came, and fetched up the ark of the LORD, and brought it into the house of Abinadab in the hill, and sanctified Eleazar his son to keep the ark of the LORD. It came to pass, while the ark abode in Kirjath-jearim, that the time was long; for it was twenty years: and all the house of Israel lamented after the LORD. Guidance: A small hilltop town receives Israel's most sacred object. Abinadab — name unknown until now — opens his home; his son Eleazar (not the Levitical high priest of the same name, but consecrated for the role) keeps watch. The ark will rest here for twenty years until David retrieves it (2 Sam 6). The chapter's hinge: 'all the house of Israel lamented after the LORD.' The Hebrew verb is the sound of a baby's wail for absent mother — the whole nation finally misses God Himself, not just His ark. Two decades of quiet ache is what it took for Israel to want the LORD back. Sometimes the loudest worship begins with the longest silence.

Samuel spake unto all the house of Israel, saying, If ye do return unto the LORD with all your hearts, then put away the strange gods and Ashtaroth from among you, and prepare your hearts unto the LORD, and serve him only: and he will deliver you out of the hand of the Philistines. Then the children of Israel did put away Baalim and Ashtaroth, and served the LORD only. Guidance: Samuel's first sermon to all Israel. Four imperatives: return, put away, prepare hearts, serve only. The condition before the promise: 'If… then he will deliver.' Repentance is not a magic password but the only door open. Samuel does not say 'put away some'; he says 'put away.' And Israel does — at least this time — the chapter's only verse where the whole nation obeys without arguing. Twenty years of grief made them tractable to the first call.

Samuel spake unto all the house of Israel, saying, If ye do return unto the LORD with all your hearts, then put away the strange gods and Ashtaroth from among you, and prepare your hearts unto the LORD, and serve him only: and he will deliver you out of the hand of the Philistines. Then the children of Israel did put away Baalim and Ashtaroth, and served the LORD only. Guidance: Samuel's first sermon to all Israel. Four imperatives: return, put away, prepare hearts, serve only. The condition before the promise: 'If… then he will deliver.' Repentance is not a magic password but the only door open. Samuel does not say 'put away some'; he says 'put away.' And Israel does — at least this time — the chapter's only verse where the whole nation obeys without arguing. Twenty years of grief made them tractable to the first call.

Samuel said, Gather all Israel to Mizpeh, and I will pray for you unto the LORD. They gathered together to Mizpeh, and drew water, and poured it out before the LORD, and fasted on that day, and said there, We have sinned against the LORD. And Samuel judged the children of Israel in Mizpeh. Guidance: The water-pouring is unusual — not commanded in any Mosaic ritual, but appears here as a symbol of pouring out the heart (cf. Lam 2:19). They are emptying themselves before God. Fasting and a public confession — 'We have sinned against the LORD' — accompany the symbolic act. Samuel 'judged' them at Mizpeh: he hears cases, mediates disputes, and reads the law. The renewal of the nation has both vertical (water, fast, confession) and horizontal (justice, judgment) dimensions. Real revival is never only spiritual; it always also reorders communal life.

They are emptying themselves before God. Fasting and a public confession — 'We have sinned against the LORD' — accompany the symbolic act. Samuel 'judged' them at Mizpeh: he hears cases, mediates disputes, and reads the law. The renewal of the nation has both vertical (water, fast, confession) and horizontal (justice, judgment) dimensions. Real revival is never only spiritual; it always also reorders communal life.

When the Philistines heard that the children of Israel were gathered together to Mizpeh, the lords of the Philistines went up against Israel. And when the children of Israel heard it, they were afraid of the Philistines. And the children of Israel said to Samuel, Cease not to cry unto the LORD our God for us, that he will save us out of the hand of the Philistines. Samuel took a sucking lamb, and offered it for a burnt offering wholly unto the LORD: and Samuel cried unto the LORD for Israel; and the LORD heard him. Guidance: A national repentance assembly looks to the Philistines like a war mobilization. They march. Israel — without weapons, gathered for worship not war — is suddenly cornered. Their response: 'Cease not to cry unto the LORD.' Note the contrast with chapter 4, when they grabbed the ark and shouted. Now they ask the prophet to pray. Samuel offers a sucking lamb 'wholly' — a complete burnt offering, nothing held back. 'The LORD heard him.' Three words; the chapter's pivot.

Guidance: A national repentance assembly looks to the Philistines like a war mobilization. They march. Israel — without weapons, gathered for worship not war — is suddenly cornered. Their response: 'Cease not to cry unto the LORD.' Note the contrast with chapter 4, when they grabbed the ark and shouted. Now they ask the prophet to pray. Samuel offers a sucking lamb 'wholly' — a complete burnt offering, nothing held back. 'The LORD heard him.' Three words; the chapter's pivot.

As Samuel was offering up the burnt offering, the Philistines drew near to battle against Israel: but the LORD thundered with a great thunder on that day upon the Philistines, and discomfited them; and they were smitten before Israel. And the men of Israel went out of Mizpeh, and pursued the Philistines, and smote them, until they came under Beth-car. Guidance: God answers exactly as Samuel is still on his knees. The smoke is still rising from the lamb when the thunder rolls. 'Thundered' is the same Hebrew verb used in Hannah's song (2:10): 'out of heaven shall he thunder upon them.' The mother's song is its son's answered prayer twenty-odd years later. The Philistines, who once exploited Israel's complacency, are now routed by Israel's worship. The chapter's reversal of chapter 4 is total: same enemy, opposite outcome, because the people inside the camp now know the difference between a relic and the LORD.

Then Samuel took a stone, and set it between Mizpeh and Shen, and called the name of it Eben-ezer, saying, Hitherto hath the LORD helped us. Guidance: Same plain where Israel was crushed in chapter 4 (Eben-ezer, 4:1). Samuel renames the geography. The stone is not for the past victory alone; it is for everything up to this point. 'Hitherto' (Heb 'ad-henah, 'as far as here') is the prayer of every survivor still on the road. God has not finished helping; the stone marks one waypoint in a long journey. Many faithful believers have quoted this verse over decades they did not expect to survive. The name does not say 'the LORD has saved us once and for all'; it says 'so far, He has been helping.' Faith's grammar is past-and-still-going.

So the Philistines were subdued, and they came no more into the coast of Israel: and the hand of the LORD was against the Philistines all the days of Samuel. And the cities which the Philistines had taken from Israel were restored to Israel, from Ekron even unto Gath; and the coasts thereof did Israel deliver out of the hand of the Philistines. And there was peace between Israel and the Amorites. Guidance: One generation of peace under Samuel. The Philistines do not invade Israel's territory again 'all the days of Samuel' — perhaps four decades. Lost cities return to Israelite hands. Peace even with the Amorites, the perennial hill-country enemy. This is the era of restoration. The narrator wants the reader to see what the LORD does when His people repent thoroughly and pray earnestly. Samuel's prayers are doing what an army of 40,000 could not. The stone in the field is the public ledger that worship beats weaponry every time it is honest.

Samuel judged Israel all the days of his life. He went from year to year in circuit to Beth-el, and Gilgal, and Mizpeh, and judged Israel in all those places. And his return was to Ramah; for there was his house; and there he judged Israel; and there he built an altar unto the LORD. Guidance: Samuel does not centralize justice in one city — he travels. Three sites yearly: Beth-el (where Jacob saw the stairway), Gilgal (where Joshua first encamped), Mizpeh (where this chapter's revival happened). Three pieces of national memory revisited every year. Then home to Ramah, where he keeps his own altar. Notice he builds an altar — Levitical irregularity (only Shiloh's altar was authorized), but the Mosaic tabernacle had been scattered by the Philistine invasion. Samuel adapts to a disrupted era. He is the bridge between Moses' system and the kings' coming temple. The mother who prayed for one son got a circuit-judge who shaped the whole nation. The chapter ends with the boy from chapter 1 traveling endlessly through Israel, holding the law together until the next era arrives.
